teaching.title: What is Recursion?
teaching.analogy: Recursion is like standing between two mirrors — you see infinite reflections. A function calls ITSELF, creating smaller copies until reaching a stopping point.
teaching.content: A recursive function calls itself with a smaller/simpler input. It MUST have a base case (stopping condition) or it will run forever and crash.

examples.title: Countdown with Recursion
examples.explanation: The function calls itself with n-1 until n=0.
examples.code:
def countdown(n):
    if n == 0:          # Base case!
        print('GO!')
        return
    print(n)
    countdown(n - 1)    # Recursive call

countdown(3)
# 3, 2, 1, GO!

teaching.funFact: The term 'recursion' is itself recursive — Google 'recursion' and it says 'Did you mean: recursion?' 😂

teaching.title: Base Case vs Recursive Case
teaching.analogy: The base case is the parachute. Without it, the recursion falls forever. The recursive case is the jump — it gets you closer to the ground each time.
teaching.content: Every recursive function needs: 1) Base case — when to STOP. 2) Recursive case — calling itself with a SIMPLER input. Without both, you get infinite recursion (stack overflow).

examples.title: Factorial
examples.code:
def factorial(n):
    if n <= 1:           # Base case
        return 1
    return n * factorial(n - 1)  # Recursive

print(factorial(5))  # 120

teaching.funFact: Python limits recursion to ~1000 calls by default. You can change it with sys.setrecursionlimit(), but deep recursion is usually a sign of bad design! 🏗️

mcq.explanation: The base case is the condition where the function stops calling itself.

mcq.q: What happens without a base case?
mcq.explanation: Without a base case, the function calls itself forever until Python's stack overflows.

mcq.q: Can every recursive function be written as a loop?
mcq.explanation: Yes — every recursion can be rewritten with loops (and vice versa). They're equivalent in power.

coding.title: Factorial
Base Case
coding.objective: In a countdown(n) function, what value of n should be the base case to stop?
coding.starterCode:
def countdown(n):
    if n == ___: return
    print(n)
    countdown(n-1)
coding.hint: Stop when you reach 0 — nothing left to count.

debugging.description:
🚨 Infinite recursion bug:

def count(n):
    print(n)
    count(n + 1)
options: Missing base case — no condition to stop, so it runs forever
debugging.answer: Missing base case — no condition to stop, so it runs forever
debugging.hint: Every recursive function needs an if condition to STOP calling itself.

teaching.title: Linear Search
teaching.analogy: Linear search is like looking for your friend in a crowd by checking every single person, one by one, from left to right.
teaching.content: Linear search checks each element in order until it finds the target or reaches the end. It works on ANY list (sorted or not). Time: O(n) — worst case checks everything.

examples.title: Linear Search

teaching.funFact: If you have 1 million items, linear search might check ALL 1 million. Binary search would check only 20! 🚀

teaching.title: Binary Search (Concept)
teaching.analogy: Binary search is like opening a dictionary to the middle. Is the word before or after? Cut the book in half each time. Way faster!
teaching.content: Binary search only works on SORTED data. It checks the middle, then eliminates half the remaining items. Repeats until found. Time: O(log n) — extremely fast!

examples.title: Binary Search Idea
examples.explanation: Sorted list only! Halves the search space each step.
examples.code:
# Searching for 7 in [1, 3, 5, 7, 9]
# Step 1: Middle = 5. 7 > 5 → go right
# Step 2: Middle = 7. Found! ✅
# Only 2 steps instead of 4!

teaching.funFact: Binary search can find any item in a billion-element sorted list in just 30 steps! 🤯

mcq.q: Linear search checks items in what order?
options: One by one from start to end
mcq.explanation: Linear search goes left to right, checking every element sequentially.

mcq.q: What does linear search return if the item isn't found?
mcq.explanation: By convention, search functions return -1 to indicate 'not found'.

mcq.q: Binary search requires the list to be ___.
mcq.explanation: Binary search ONLY works on sorted data. It relies on order to eliminate half each step.
Binary: log₂(1000) ≈ 10 checks. Linear: up to 1000 checks. Binary wins hugely!

coding.title: Not Found
coding.starterCode:
# [1, 2, 3], target: 5
# 5 is not in the list
# Returns: ___
coding.hint: 5 is not in the list, so it returns -1.

options: Return -1 for not found (0 is a valid index!)
debugging.answer: Return -1 for not found (0 is a valid index!)
debugging.hint: Returning 0 is misleading because 0 is a valid index (first element).

teaching.title: Bubble Sort
teaching.analogy: Bubble Sort is like bubbles rising in water. The biggest number 'bubbles up' to the end in each pass. Repeat until everything is sorted.
teaching.content: Bubble Sort compares adjacent pairs and swaps them if they're in the wrong order. After one pass, the largest is at the end. Repeat for remaining items.

examples.title: Bubble Sort Step
examples.explanation: Compare neighbors, swap if wrong order.

teaching.funFact: Bubble Sort is very slow (O(n²)) but easy to understand. No one uses it in real software — it's purely educational! 🎓

teaching.title: Swapping — The Core Move
teaching.analogy: Swapping is like trading cards with a friend. You each put your card face down, then pick up the other's card. In Python, it's even simpler!
teaching.content: To swap two variables, Python can do it in one line: a, b = b, a. This is the fundamental operation in sorting algorithms.

examples.title: Python Swap
examples.explanation: One line swaps both values instantly!
examples.code:
a = 5
b = 10
a, b = b, a   # Swap!
print(a, b)   # 10 5

# In a list:
arr = [3, 1]
arr[0], arr[1] = arr[1], arr[0]
print(arr)  # [1, 3]

teaching.funFact: In C/Java you need a temporary variable to swap. Python's tuple unpacking makes it a one-liner! 🐍

mcq.explanation: Bubble Sort compares neighboring pairs and swaps if needed.

mcq.q: After 1 pass of Bubble Sort, which element is in its final position?
mcq.explanation: Each pass 'bubbles' the largest remaining element to its correct position at the end.

mcq.q: How do you swap a and b in Python?
mcq.explanation: a, b = b, a is Python's clean one-line swap using tuple unpacking.

options: No, it's O(n²) — one of the slowest
mcq.explanation: O(n²) means the time grows rapidly. For 1000 items, that's 1,000,000 comparisons!

coding.title: Swap Output
coding.objective: a=7, b=3. After a,b = b,a. What is a?
coding.starterCode:
a = 7
b = 3
a, b = b, a
print(a)
# Output: ___
coding.hint: After swap, a gets b's old value (3).

coding.title: After 1 Pass
coding.objective: [4, 2, 7, 1]. After 1 full pass of Bubble Sort, what's the last element?
coding.hint: The largest (7) bubbles to the end after one pass.

debugging.description:
🚨 Bug:

a = 5
b = 10
a = b
b = a
print(a, b)
debugging.brokenCode:
a = 5
b = 10
a = b
b = a
print(a, b)
options: After a=b, both are 10. Then b=a → both 10. Use a,b = b,a instead!
debugging.answer: After a=b, both are 10. Then b=a → both 10. Use a,b = b,a instead!
debugging.hint: When a=b runs, a becomes 10. Now b=a → b becomes 10 too. Both are 10!

prediction.code:
arr = [3, 1, 4, 1, 5]
arr[0], arr[1] = arr[1], arr[0]
print(arr)

teaching.title: ⚠️ BOSS — THE STRATEGIST
teaching.analogy: The Strategist is the ultimate tactician. It wields recursion, searches, and sorts as weapons. Only a true problem solver can defeat it!
teaching.content: This is the final boss before the Ultimate Clash. It tests recursion, base cases, linear/binary search concepts, bubble sort, and swapping.

examples.title: Your full arsenal
examples.code:
✅ Recursion & base cases
✅ Linear search & binary search
✅ Bubble sort & swapping
✅ Time complexity basics
✅ Tracing recursive calls

teaching.funFact: Google's PageRank algorithm (which sorts the entire internet's pages by relevance) was invented by two PhD students in a garage! 🏠

mcq.q: What are the 2 required parts of every recursive function?
mcq.explanation: Base case (when to stop) and recursive case (call itself with simpler input).

mcq.q: Linear search on [4,8,2,6] for 2: how many comparisons?

mcq.explanation: Binary search ONLY works on sorted data.

mcq.q: After Bubble Sort, where does the smallest element end up?
mcq.explanation: After complete Bubble Sort, smallest is at index 0 (beginning).

mcq.q: def f(n): return n*f(n-1) if n>1 else 1. What is f(4)?

coding.title: Boss: Recursive Sum
coding.objective: def sum_to(n): if n==0: return 0; return n+sum_to(n-1). What is sum_to(5)?

options: Should return the INDEX (use enumerate or range), not the value itself
debugging.answer: Should return the INDEX (use enumerate or range), not the value itself
debugging.hint: Search functions typically return the INDEX where the item was found, not the item itself.
